question: If an Architect is hired to design a Supermarket with construction cost of less than ₱50M, how much should he/she charge for Detailed Architectural and Engineering Design Services, or DAEDS, based on the recommended professional fee?
keyMemory: For DAEDS, ordinary commercial projects like a supermarket are commonly answered as 7% in ALE review context.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. 7%.
Ulitin natin: 7%.
A supermarket is a commercial building type with average complexity. Under the recommended professional fee classification for Detailed Architectural and Engineering Design Services, or DAEDS, projects of this type are commonly placed in the group using 7% as the recommended professional fee.
Important distinction: DAEDS is not just architectural drawings. It includes detailed architectural and engineering design services, meaning the architect and the needed allied professionals coordinate to produce the full design package.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. 5% — too low for DAEDS in this classification.
b. 6% — closer to simpler project types, but a supermarket is generally more complex than the simplest group.
d. 8% — usually for more complex or special building types like hospitals, theaters, or highly specialized facilities.
c. 7% — best answer for a supermarket under this reviewer classification.
Elimination method:
Keyword: Supermarket.
Not the simplest industrial/storage type, but not highly specialized like a hospital or theater. So it falls in the middle commercial classification. Middle classification = 7%.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
For DAEDS, ordinary commercial projects like a supermarket are commonly answered as 7% in ALE review context.

question: A client wanted your services for his new house with a total project cost of Three Million, ₱3,000,000.00. What is the percentage of your professional fee?
keyMemory: For ordinary residential projects under Group 4, the recommended professional fee is 10% of Project Construction Cost.
explanation:
Correct answer: b. 10%.
Ulitin natin: 10% ang professional fee for residences.
Ang clue dito ay new house. Sa classification sa page, Group 4 ang residences: single-detached, single-attached, duplex, row-houses, shop-houses, small apartment houses, and townhouses.
For Group 4 residences, the recommended professional fee is 10% of Project Construction Cost for DAEDS, or Detailed Architectural and Engineering Design Services.
So kung project construction cost is ₱3,000,000, ang 10% professional fee would be:
₱3,000,000 × 10% = ₱300,000
Bakit mali ang ibang choices?
a. 8% — hindi ito ang listed RPF for residences sa table.
c. 12% — ito ay for Group 5, like monumental buildings and special facilities. Too high for ordinary residence.
d. 15% — wala sa table for this type of residential project.
b. 10% — tama, because residences are Group 4.
Elimination method:
Una, identify the building type: house.
Second, locate the group: Group 4, residences.
Third, match the fee: 10% of PCC.
Huwag mong i-base sa amount na ₱3M alone. Ang amount helps compute the peso fee, pero ang percentage depends on the building classification.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
For ordinary residential projects under Group 4, the recommended professional fee is 10% of Project Construction Cost.

visualReviewNote: Professional Practice subject: no images needed. Text/audio-ready explanation only.

question: Mr. Juan Zubiri commissioned you for a restaurant project last year. Due to the success of the restaurant, he wants you to do a 2nd restaurant using the same design. What would be your fee?
keyMemory: For reuse of the same design: 1st = 100%, 2nd = 50%, 3rd = 40%, 4th onward = 30% of basic fee.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. 50% of basic fee.
Ulitin natin: For the 2nd building using the same design, the fee is 50% of the basic fee.
Ang clue dito ay 2nd restaurant using the same design. Ibig sabihin, reuse of plans and contract documents for another similar building.
Sa table ng Group 6, kapag re-used ang plans/design and related contract documents for repetitive construction of similar buildings without amending drawings and specifications, ganito ang fee schedule:
First building — 100% of basic fee
Second building — 50% of basic fee
Third building — 40% of basic fee
Fourth and succeeding buildings — 30% of basic fee
Since ang tanong ay 2nd restaurant, sagot ay 50% of basic fee.
Bakit mali ang ibang choices?
a. 60% — walang ganitong rate sa listed reuse schedule.
b. 80% — wala rin sa schedule.
d. 40% — ito ay for the 3rd building, not the 2nd.
c. 50% — tama, because it is the second use of the same design.
Elimination method:
Hanapin ang keyword: 2nd and same design.
Kapag same design, reuse schedule ang gamitin.
2nd building = 50%.
3rd building = 40%.
4th and succeeding = 30%.
Common trap dito: baka piliin mo 40% kasi akala mo discounted na agad. Pero tandaan, 40% is for the third building, not the second.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
For reuse of the same design: 1st = 100%, 2nd = 50%, 3rd = 40%, 4th onward = 30% of basic fee.
visualReviewNote: Professional Practice subject: no images needed. Text/audio-ready explanation only.

question: The architect’s fee for repetitive housing, 3rd unit?
keyMemory: Kapag repetitive housing, use the Group 7 bracket. 2nd to 10th unit = 50% of RPF for each additional unit. Kaya ang 3rd unit = 50%, hindi 40%.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. 50%.
Ulitin natin: For repetitive housing, the 3rd unit is charged at 50% of the recommended professional fee.
Ito ang tamang sagot kasi ang tinutukoy dito ay Group 7: Repetitive Housing. Sa table sa page, ang rule ay:
1st unit — 10% of Project Construction Cost
2nd to 10th unit — fee for one unit plus 50% of RPF for each additional unit
11th to 30th unit — 40% of RPF for each additional unit
31st to 50th unit — 25% of RPF for each additional unit
51st unit and more — 15% of RPF for each additional unit
So dahil ang tanong ay 3rd unit, pasok siya sa bracket na 2nd to 10th unit. Therefore, 50%.
Bakit hindi 40%?
Ito ang common trap. Naalala mo siguro sa previous page, sa reuse of same design for another building:
1st building = 100%
2nd building = 50%
3rd building = 40%
4th and succeeding = 30%
Pero iba iyon. That was Group 6, reuse of plans/design for repetitive construction of similar buildings.
Dito naman ang tanong ay repetitive housing, which is Group 7. At sa Group 7, ang 3rd unit ay still under the 2nd to 10th unit bracket, kaya 50%, not 40%.
Bakit mali ang ibang choices?
a. 80% — walang ganitong bracket sa repetitive housing schedule.
b. 60% — wala rin sa table.
d. 40% — ito ang trap. Sa Group 7, 40% starts only from the 11th to 30th unit. Hindi siya para sa 3rd unit.
c. 50% — tama, because the 3rd unit is within the 2nd to 10th unit range.
Elimination method:
Una, basahin ang keyword: repetitive housing.
Huwag mong ihalo sa “same design, 2nd restaurant” question from the previous page.
For repetitive housing, hanapin ang unit bracket.
3rd unit falls under 2nd to 10th unit.
So answer: 50%.

question: After all the Contract Documents have been given to the Owner, how much is the liability from the remaining professional fee?
keyMemory: Kapag natapos at naibigay na ang contract documents, isipin mo: 85% earned, 15% remaining.
explanation:
Correct answer: a. 15%.
Ulitin natin: 15% ang remaining professional fee after Contract Documents.
Ito ang tamang sagot kasi sa regular design services, kapag natapos na ang Contract Documents Phase, meaning naibigay na ang complete working drawings, specifications, schedules, and other contract documents, the architect is generally entitled to 85% of the professional fee.
So kung 85% ang earned fee, ang natitira from the full 100% professional fee is:
100% − 85% = 15%
Iyan ang ibig sabihin ng remaining professional fee dito.
Bakit mali ang iba?
b. 10% — ito ay magiging tama lang kung ang earned stage is 90%. Pero sa contract documents completion, board exam logic usually uses 85%, so remaining is 15%.
c. 5% — too small. This is more like mobilization percentage, not remaining after contract documents.
d. None of the above — mali dahil may exact answer: 15%.
Elimination method:
Keyword: Contract Documents have been given to the Owner.
Contract Documents completion = 85% earned.
Remaining balance = 15%.
Kaya sagot: 15%.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Kapag natapos at naibigay na ang contract documents, isipin mo: 85% earned, 15% remaining.

question: A client wanted your services for his new house with a total project cost of ₱3,000,000.00. Will the percentage of your fee change if the client decides to postpone the project for the following year? How much will you get if the client decides to defer the start of the construction for a year?
answer: Yes, the fee changes. The adjusted fee becomes 11.5% of the Project Construction Cost, or ₱345,000.00.
keyMemory: For delayed construction beyond six months, hindi buong fee ang dinodoble. Ang dinodoble ay construction phase service portion. For a 10% residential fee, adjusted fee becomes 11.5%.
explanation:
Correct answer: Yes, the fee changes. The adjusted fee becomes 11.5% of the Project Construction Cost, or ₱345,000.00.
Ulitin natin: yes, magbabago ang fee; magiging 11.5%, equivalent to ₱345,000.
Step-by-step tayo.
For a house, the basic recommended professional fee is 10% of Project Construction Cost.
So original fee:
₱3,000,000 × 10% = ₱300,000
Now, bakit magiging 11.5%?
Kasi kapag ang construction is postponed beyond the allowed suspension period, particularly beyond six months, the architect’s construction phase services may be charged differently. The construction phase portion is the remaining 15% of the architect’s basic fee.
So:
Basic architect’s fee = ₱300,000
Construction phase portion = 15% of ₱300,000
15% × ₱300,000 = ₱45,000
Kapag delayed for one year, the construction phase service is doubled. So instead of ₱45,000, magiging:
₱45,000 × 2 = ₱90,000
Now compute total:
Pre-construction/design phases = 85% of ₱300,000 = ₱255,000
Doubled construction phase = ₱90,000
Total adjusted fee = ₱255,000 + ₱90,000 = ₱345,000
As percentage of project cost:
₱345,000 ÷ ₱3,000,000 = 11.5%
Bakit hindi 10% lang? Kasi 10% assumes normal continuous project delivery. Kapag delayed for one year, extended ang architect’s responsibility and services during construction phase.
Bakit hindi 20%? Kasi hindi buong professional fee ang dinodoble. Ang dinodoble lang ay construction phase service portion, not the whole basic fee.
Elimination method:
Una, identify building type: house = 10% basic fee.
Second, identify delay: one year, more than six months.
Third, only the construction phase portion is affected.
Construction phase portion = 15% of PF.
So total fee becomes 115% of the basic fee, or 11.5% of PCC.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
For delayed construction beyond six months, hindi buong fee ang dinodoble. Ang dinodoble ay construction phase service portion. For a 10% residential fee, adjusted fee becomes 11.5%.

question: If the owner wants to build the house after one year, how much is the fee?
keyMemory: For a delayed residential project after one year, the fee is 11.5%, not the normal 10%.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. 11.5%.
Ulitin natin: 11.5% ang fee.
Ito ay continuation ng previous computation. Normal residential professional fee is 10% of Project Construction Cost.
Pero dahil after one year pa itutuloy ang construction, affected ang construction phase service. Since the construction phase portion is 15% of the architect’s professional fee, and that portion is doubled due to delay beyond six months, the total becomes:
100% basic fee + additional 15% of basic fee = 115% of basic fee
Since basic fee is 10% of PCC:
10% × 115% = 11.5%
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. 10% — normal fee ito, but it ignores the one-year delay.
b. 10.5% — too low. It assumes only 5% increase, but the construction phase portion is 15%.
c. 11% — close but still short. It would imply only 10% additional on the fee, but the actual construction phase portion is 15%.
d. 11.5% — tama.
Elimination method:
Keyword: after one year.
That means delay beyond six months.
Residential fee = 10%.
Construction phase portion = 15% of PF.
Add that 15% portion to the basic fee: 10% becomes 11.5%.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
For a delayed residential project after one year, the fee is 11.5%, not the normal 10%.

question: Owner A hired Architect B to design his condominium located in a commercial area. After several meetings, Architect B was able to produce a Schematic Design which Owner A subsequently approved. However, Owner A had a change of heart and decided that he doesn’t want Architect B for the project anymore. How much compensation is Architect B entitled to?

choices.text: 15% of the PF plus 5% for Breach of Contract

keyMemory: Once the schematic design is completed and approved, the architect is entitled to 20% of the professional fee, even if the owner later changes his mind.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. 20% of the PF.
Ulitin natin: Architect B is entitled to 20% of the professional fee.
Bakit? Kasi natapos at na-approve na ang Schematic Design Phase. Sa progress payment schedule, completion of the schematic design stage corresponds to 20% of the professional fee, usually cumulative from the start of the agreement.
Ang important clue dito ay “Schematic Design which Owner A subsequently approved.” Approved na siya. Ibig sabihin, may professional service already rendered and accepted by the owner. Kahit nagbago ang isip ng owner, hindi mabubura ang work na nagawa na.
Bakit mali ang ibang choices?
a. 15% of PF plus 5% for breach of contract — tempting, kasi 15% plus 5% equals 20%. Pero wrong reasoning. Hindi natin kailangang tawaging “breach of contract penalty” unless stated sa agreement. Ang proper basis is progress completion: schematic design = 20%.
b. 15% of PF — kulang. Kung schematic design phase has been completed and approved, total entitlement is 20%, not merely 15%.
d. 10% of PF — too low. Wala itong match sa schematic design completion.
c. 20% of PF — tama.
Elimination method:
Keyword: approved schematic design.
Schematic design completion = 20% of PF.
Huwag madala sa “change of heart.” Owner may stop the project, but he must pay for services already rendered.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Once the schematic design is completed and approved, the architect is entitled to 20% of the professional fee, even if the owner later changes his mind.

question: Architect Guevarra was commissioned to do a residential project. Everything worked out until the contract documents were done except that the owner ran out of budget to start the construction. According to the law, how much is Arch. Guevarra entitled to collect?
keyMemory: Kapag natapos ang contract documents but construction does not proceed, the architect is entitled to 85% of the professional fee.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. 85%.
Ulitin natin: Architect Guevarra is entitled to collect 85% of the professional fee.
Bakit? Kasi ang work niya umabot hanggang Contract Documents Phase. Ibig sabihin, tapos na ang detailed plans, working drawings, specifications, and other documents needed for construction.
Hindi pa nagsimula ang construction, so hindi pa niya na-render ang full construction phase services. Kaya hindi pa 100%.
Hindi rin 90%, kasi 90% is typically associated with later pre-construction/bidding-related progress, like after bidding or award stage depending on the payment schedule. Dito, ang facts say contract documents were done, but construction did not start because the owner had no budget. So the clean answer is 85%.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. 80% — too low; contract documents are already completed.
b. 90% — too high for only contract documents if no bidding/award/construction phase proceeded.
c. 100% — wrong because the entire architectural service has not been completed. Construction phase services were not rendered.
d. 85% — tama, because Contract Documents Phase completion earns 85% of PF.
Elimination method:
Keyword: contract documents were done.
Contract documents done = 85%.
No construction started = not 100%.
No further bidding/award stage indicated = not 90%.
So answer: 85%.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Kapag natapos ang contract documents but construction does not proceed, the architect is entitled to 85% of the professional fee.

question: An architect makes a miniature model of the project for his own design study. Who should pay for it?
keyMemory: Model for architect’s own study = Architect pays. Model requested for owner’s approval, presentation, or sales = Owner pays separately.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. Architect.
Ulitin natin: Architect ang magbabayad.

Kapag ang model ay internal tool ng architect para sa sarili niyang study, hindi siya automatic chargeable to the owner. Part siya ng architect’s design process.
Pero important distinction: kung ang scale model, 3D model, or walk-through presentation ay required or requested by the owner for approval, presentation, sales, or marketing, puwede itong i-charge separately. Pero dito, ang keyword ay for his own design study. Kaya architect ang cost.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Project Manager — walang relation. Hindi siya ang gumagawa ng model for the architect’s design study.
b. Contractor — mali. Wala pa sa construction issue.
c. Owner — hindi best answer kasi hindi owner ang nag-request. Internal design study ito.
d. Architect — tama, dahil siya ang gumamit ng model for his own design work.
Elimination method:
Keyword: for his own design study.
Kapag sariling study ng architect, sariling design tool niya iyon.
Kung owner-requested presentation model, owner pays separately.
Pero kung architect’s internal design model, architect pays.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Model for architect’s own study = Architect pays.
Model requested for owner’s approval, presentation, or sales = Owner pays separately.

question: You have been chosen as architect of a complex project for a big corporation. question: Who determines time and cost for the project?
keyMemory: The Owner determines project time and cost objectives. The professionals help manage, control, and advise.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. Owner.
Ulitin natin: the Owner determines the time and cost for the project.
Bakit owner? Kasi the owner is the one who sets the project objectives: budget, desired completion period, project priorities, and business constraints. The professionals advise, plan, estimate, control, and manage, pero the owner ultimately defines the project’s target cost and time expectations.
The architect, project manager, or construction manager may help evaluate whether those targets are realistic. Pero kung sino ang nagtatakda ng budget and desired timeline as project goals, that is the owner.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Project Manager — manages and coordinates time and cost, but does not own the project objectives.
b. Construction Manager — controls and monitors construction time and cost, but still works within owner-approved targets.
d. Contractor — bids and constructs according to contract time and cost, but does not determine the owner’s project budget and objectives.
Elimination method:
Tanungin mo: “Sino ang may project? Sino ang may budget? Sino ang magdedesisyon kung magkano kaya niya at kailan niya gusto matapos?”
Answer: Owner.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
The Owner determines project time and cost objectives. The professionals help manage, control, and advise.

question: Who is responsible for shortening the time and lessening the cost of the project?

The architect designs and administers professional services. The contractor constructs. The client owns and funds. Pero yung active responsibility to manage time and cost efficiency across the project is closest to the Project Manager.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Architect — may role siya in design efficiency and coordination, pero hindi siya automatically the one responsible for overall time and cost reduction unless hired as project manager.
c. Contractor — may duty to finish within contract time and cost, pero broad project optimization is not solely contractor’s role.
d. Client — sets goals and approves decisions, but does not professionally manage the schedule and cost reduction process.
Elimination method:
Keyword: shortening time and lessening cost.
That is project management language.
So answer: Project Manager.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
The owner sets time and cost goals. The Project Manager helps manage the project to reduce time and control cost.

So sa choices, Client ang best answer.
Bakit hindi architect? Kasi hindi ang architect ang owner ng project. The architect assists. Hindi siya automatic ang applicant-owner.
Bakit hindi engineer? Engineer signs and seals engineering documents under his discipline, but does not generally secure the permit as the project owner.
Bakit hindi contractor? Contractor may help process permits in some arrangements, pero the legal applicant or responsible project owner is still the owner/client.
Elimination method:
Keyword: secure building permit.
Ask: “Kanino ang project? Sino ang applicant-owner?” Client/Owner.
Architect assists, but client secures.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
The Owner/Client secures permits, while the architect assists in preparing and filing the required documents.

Owner A, to be paid directly by him

Bakit owner? Kasi kapag kailangan ng separate specialist or allied consultant — for example acoustics for a large function hall — the cost of that specialist is a separate service required for the project. Hindi dapat ibawas automatically sa architect’s fee, unless malinaw sa contract na kasama iyon. Kung hindi included sa basic professional fee, separate compensation siya.

b. Owner A pays directly — tama.

question: Which is not a Structural Plan?

The other choices are actual structural plans:
Foundation Plan — plan showing footings, columns, foundation layout.
Floor Framing Plan — plan showing beams, girders, slabs, framing members at floor level.
Roof Framing Plan — plan showing roof structural framing members.
So the question is not asking “which is not structural?” It asks which is not a structural plan. That’s why the answer is Schedule of Beams.

Look at the word Plan.
Foundation Plan? Plan.
Floor Framing Plan? Plan.
Roof Framing Plan? Plan.
Schedule of Beams? Structural schedule, but not a plan.
So answer: Schedule of Beams.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
A schedule can be a structural document, but it is not a plan. Foundation, floor framing, and roof framing are structural plans.

question: Which is not part of Architectural Design Drawings?

c. Schedule of Electrical Loads — clearly electrical, not architectural.

Hanapin mo yung item na pinaka-layo sa architectural drawing set. Electrical loads? That belongs to electrical design. So answer: C.

Floor plans and roof plans are architectural. Electrical load schedules belong to electrical documents, not architectural design drawings.

question: The owner likes a particular bidder; he is delayed and fails to beat the deadline. What factors can be considered so that he can still join the bid?

choices.text: Continue with bidding but declare no winner

keyMemory: In bidding, fairness beats preference. Hindi puwedeng baguhin ang proseso dahil may favored bidder.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. Nothing.
Ulitin natin: Nothing.
Bakit? Kasi bidding must be fair, transparent, and equal to all bidders. Kung may deadline, lahat dapat sumusunod. Hindi puwedeng dahil gusto ng owner ang isang bidder, bibigyan siya ng special treatment after missing the deadline.
Kung papayagan ang late bidder dahil “favorite” siya, unfair iyon sa ibang bidders na sumunod sa rules. That defeats the purpose of bidding.

a. Reschedule bidding — mali kung ang only reason ay para maka-join ang favored bidder. That is unfair.

c. Continue with bidding but declare no winner — unethical kung ginagawa lang para ma-accommodate ang preferred bidder.
d. Nothing — tama. Late is late. Follow the rules.
Elimination method:
Keyword: owner likes a particular bidder. Red flag yan. Favoritism.
Bidding rules must apply equally. Kaya no special accommodation. Answer: Nothing.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
In bidding, fairness beats preference. Hindi puwedeng baguhin ang proseso dahil may favored bidder.

question: If the owner wants to change the estimated cost after awarding to a winning bidder due to economic basis, what should he do?
keyMemory: To change cost properly, revise the scope of work/contract, not the fairness of the bidding process.
explanation:
Correct answer: b. Revise scope of contract.
Ulitin natin: Revise the scope of contract.
Bakit? Kapag may economic reason and the owner wants to reduce or change the project cost after award, the proper professional move is not to change the architect, not to manipulate bidding, and not automatically to rebid. The logical move is to revise or adjust the scope of work.
For example, reduce floor area, change finishes, simplify systems, remove non-essential items, or value-engineer certain elements. Kapag nagbago ang scope, doon bababa or mababago ang contract cost.
Bakit hindi change order? Change order usually applies after a contract is already in effect and during construction, when adding, deleting, or changing work. Dito, ang wording is after awarding and due to economic basis, so the more basic correction is revise the scope of contract.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Change architect — walang kinalaman sa cost adjustment.
c. Change order — possible later during construction, but not the best answer for adjusting award scope due to economics.
d. Organize another bidding — not the first remedy, and may be unfair if only done to manipulate cost.
b. Revise scope of contract — correct.
Elimination method:
If cost is too high, ask: “Ano ang babaguhin para bumaba ang cost?”
Hindi tao. Hindi favorite bidder. Hindi agad rebid.
Ang babaguhin ay scope. Kaya B.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
To change cost properly, revise the scope of work/contract, not the fairness of the bidding process.

question: If during the excavation, you found out that the lot actually overlaps with the adjacent property, what will you do?

choices.text: You’re in trouble. This should have been discovered early in the first place.
Negotiate with the owner of the adjacent property.
Make the necessary adjustments so that they don’t overlap.
Do nothing. It’s up to the neighbor to discover.

answer: C. Make the necessary adjustments so that they don’t overlap.
keyMemory: Any encroachment into adjacent property must be corrected. Always verify boundaries early through proper survey, and if discovered during excavation, stop, document, and adjust before proceeding.
explanation:
Correct answer: c. Make the necessary adjustments so that they don’t overlap.
Ulitin natin: Make the necessary adjustments so that they don’t overlap.
Bakit? Kapag nalaman during excavation na may overlap or encroachment sa adjacent property, hindi puwedeng ipagpatuloy na parang walang nangyari. Kailangan itigil or correct ang affected work, verify the property line, coordinate with the owner and professionals, and adjust the construction/layout so it stays within the proper property boundaries.
In real practice, kailangan ng proper survey, documentation, notice to the owner, and possibly legal coordination. Pero among the choices, ang best professional action is to make the necessary adjustments.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. You’re in trouble — may truth na dapat nadetect earlier through survey, pero this is not a solution. Board exams usually ask the corrective action.
b. Negotiate with the adjacent owner — possible later if there is a legal boundary issue, but the immediate professional response is not to rely on negotiation. First, avoid overlap and correct the work.
d. Do nothing — clearly wrong and unethical. That may lead to encroachment, dispute, damages, or legal liability.
c. Make necessary adjustments — best answer.
Elimination method:
Keyword: overlaps with adjacent property.
Do nothing? Impossible.
Blame yourself? Not corrective.
Negotiate? Maybe later, but not the primary construction response.
Correct the work so it does not overlap. Kaya C.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Any encroachment into adjacent property must be corrected. Always verify boundaries early through proper survey, and if discovered during excavation, stop, document, and adjust before proceeding.

question: What is the role of the Architect in the bidding?

choices.text: Award the project to the Contractor

keyMemory: In bidding, the architect assists and advises. The owner awards.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. Observe the proceedings.
Ulitin natin: the Architect observes the proceedings and assists the Owner during bidding.
Bakit ito ang sagot? Kasi sa bidding, hindi ang architect ang final awarding authority. Ang owner ang may power to award the project. The architect’s role is to assist: prepare bidding documents, help answer questions, evaluate bids, prepare bid abstracts, and advise the owner. Pero hindi siya ang may final right to award.
Kaya ang pinaka-safe answer sa choices is observe the proceedings. It means the architect is present professionally, making sure the process is properly conducted, and helping the owner understand the bids.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Award the project to the Contractor — mali. Owner ang nag-a-award, not the architect.
b. Organize the bidding — possible na tumulong ang architect, pero hindi siya necessarily ang organizer or authority. The architect assists, but does not own the bidding process.
c. Choose the Construction Manager — unrelated ito. The construction manager is usually selected by the owner.
d. Observe the proceedings — tama, kasi the architect assists and observes, but does not replace the owner’s authority.
Elimination method:
Kapag bidding, tanungin mo: “Sino ang may pera at project ownership?” Owner.
So tanggal agad “award the project.”
Architect helps technically, pero owner decides.
Kaya ang role niya sa choices: observe the proceedings.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
In bidding, the architect assists and advises. The owner awards.

question: When should a Building Operations Manual or BOM be submitted to the owner by the contractor?

Kasama rito ang manuals, warranties, as-built drawings, guarantees, directory of panels and circuits, and other turnover documents. Hindi dapat final payment muna bago ibigay ang manual. Baliktad iyon. Dapat may complete turnover muna bago final release.
Bakit mali ang iba?

c. As soon as building is finished and ready for occupancy — close, pero mas precise ang contractual answer: before final payment.
d. Before final payment is made — tama.
Elimination method:
Keyword: submitted to the owner by the contractor. Think close-out requirement. Close-out documents must be completed before final payment.

question: When can a contractor terminate a contract?
answer: The contractor may terminate after 15 days’ written notice to the Owner and Architect, under valid conditions such as prolonged suspension, non-action on payment requests, or non-payment after arbitration.

Sa page, listed ang conditions. The contractor may suspend work or terminate the contract upon 15 days written notice to the Owner and Architect if, for example:
First, work is suspended for 90 days through no fault of the contractor.
Second, the architect fails to act on a request for payment within 15 days.
Third, the owner fails to act on a request for payment within 15 days.
Fourth, the owner fails to pay any sum within 30 days after award by arbitration.
Bakit important ito? Kasi hindi puwedeng basta mag-walk out ang contractor. May process. May notice. May valid ground.
Elimination method kapag lumabas ito as MCQ:
Hanapin ang words na 15 days written notice and valid causes like 90-day suspension, failure to act on payment, or failure to pay arbitration award.

A contractor cannot just abandon work. Termination needs valid cause plus 15 days written notice to the Owner and Architect.

question: Which is not a qualification of a specs writer?

choices.text: Degree and experience in construction management
Degree and experience in latest construction trends and materials

keyMemory: A specs writer needs knowledge, training, and experience. References are helpful tools, but a library of references is not itself a qualification.
explanation:
Best answer: c. Library of references.
Ulitin natin: Library of references is not a qualification.
Bakit ito ang best answer? Kasi a qualification is something about the person’s competence, education, training, experience, and knowledge. A library of references is useful, yes, pero it is a tool or resource, not a qualification of the writer.
A good specs writer should have knowledge of construction, materials, methods, standards, codes, and current products. The specs writer may also coordinate with suppliers or manufacturers to obtain technical information, but professional judgment must remain independent.
Bakit mali or weaker ang iba?
a. Degree and experience in construction management — this can support specs writing because specifications relate to construction process, documentation, and implementation.
b. Contact with suppliers — not a formal credential, pero useful for obtaining product information. Dapat lang hindi biased or influenced by commissions.
d. Degree and experience in latest construction trends and materials — awkward ang wording, pero knowledge and experience with current materials and construction trends are relevant to specs writing.
c. Library of references — pinaka-clear na hindi personal qualification; it is a resource.
Elimination method:
Ask: “Alin dito ang trait or competence ng specs writer?” Education, experience, knowledge, industry contact can relate to competence.
“Alin ang gamit lang or collection of materials?” Library of references.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
A specs writer needs knowledge, training, and experience. References are helpful tools, but a library of references is not itself a qualification.

question: What is the purpose of the Bill of Materials?
keyMemory: BOM is mainly a cost reference, not the construction instruction itself.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. Reference on Cost.
Ulitin natin: Bill of Materials is a reference on cost.
Ang Bill of Materials, or BOM, gives a listing of the materials and quantities needed for the project. Ginagamit ito para magkaroon ng cost reference or basis for estimating. Hindi siya simpleng drawing, and hindi rin siya contract award by itself. It helps the owner and project team understand the probable cost implication of the materials.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Bidding — may use siya sa bidding, yes, pero hindi iyon ang main purpose sa choices.
b. Loan — possible na gamitin as support for loan evaluation, pero hindi siya created mainly for loan.
c. Construction Guide — medyo close, pero mas exact ang cost reference. Construction guide is more drawings and specifications.
d. Reference on Cost — tama.
Elimination method:
Kapag nakita mo Bill of Materials, isipin mo: materials + quantities = cost estimate reference. Kaya reference on cost ang best answer.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
BOM is mainly a cost reference, not the construction instruction itself.

question: To whom will the owner most likely award the project?

choices.text: Bid with the lowest labor cost
Bid with the lowest construction cost
Bid with the lowest material cost
Bid with the lowest contingency cost

answer: B. Bid with the lowest construction cost.
keyMemory: Sa bidding, compare the total construction cost, not isolated cost components.
explanation:
Correct answer: b. Bid with the lowest construction cost.
Ulitin natin: the owner will most likely award to the bid with the lowest construction cost.
Ang bidding is not based on labor cost alone, material cost alone, or contingency alone. Ang owner looks at the total construction bid or total construction cost, assuming the bidder is qualified and responsive.
Important: Hindi always “lowest” in real life kung hindi qualified or non-responsive ang bidder. Pero among the choices, ang best answer is lowest construction cost, not just one component.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Lowest labor cost — too narrow. Labor is only one part of the bid.
c. Lowest material cost — too narrow din.
d. Lowest contingency cost — contingency is only allowance, not the whole bid.
b. Lowest construction cost — best answer.
Elimination method:
Tanggal lahat ng partial cost: labor, material, contingency. Ang award is based on the whole construction bid. Kaya lowest construction cost.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Sa bidding, compare the total construction cost, not isolated cost components.

question: Architect A is the principal Architect of an architectural firm. Architects B & C are Junior Partners and they are in-charge of the Design Team. However, it is still Architect A who signs and seals all the drawings. Recently, they hired Architects D & E, both RLAs, to be in-charge of the Production Team. What will be the position of Architects D & E?
keyMemory: Licensed architects working in a firm but not partners are usually treated as Associate Architects in this context.
explanation:
Correct answer: d. Associate Architects.
Ulitin natin: Architects D and E are Associate Architects.
Bakit? D and E are both RLAs, meaning Registered and Licensed Architects. They are not mere draftsmen. They were hired into the firm and placed in charge of the production team, but the question does not say they are partners or owners. So the best position is Associate Architects.
Bakit mali ang iba?
a. Draftsmen — mali, kasi RLAs sila. Draftsmen may produce drawings, but the question identifies them as licensed architects.
b. Junior Architects — weaker answer. In many office contexts, junior architects may be less experienced or not necessarily licensed.
c. Junior Partners — mali, kasi hindi sinabi na may ownership or partnership interest sila.
d. Associate Architects — tama, licensed architects in the firm but not principal or partners.
Elimination method:
RLA sila, so tanggal draftsmen.
Hindi sila partners, so tanggal junior partners.
Between junior architect and associate architect, mas bagay ang Associate Architects dahil licensed professionals sila handling production.
Ano ang dapat mong tandaan dito?
Licensed architects working in a firm but not partners are usually treated as Associate Architects in this context.

question: Who is liable for the drawings under the law?

a. Arch. A — tama.
Elimination method:
Hanapin ang phrase: signs and seals all the drawings. Whoever signs and seals, siya ang liable. Kaya Arch. A.
